data.title: OPKO Health authorizes $100 million increase to share buyback program
data.description:
• OPKO Health (NASDAQ:OPK) said its board has authorized an increase of $100 million to the company’s existing common stock repurchase program.
  • The capacity of the current program now stood at $200 million.
  • The firm added ~$41 million of its common stock has been repurchased under the existing program since its authorization in July 2024.
  • The company added it will fund the buyback from existing cash and cash equivalents, and future cash flows.

data.title: Starbucks falls again as the giant tariff overhang clouds the outlook
data.description:
Starbucks Corporation (NASDAQ:SBUX) continues to spiral lower after the Trump Administration's announcement on tariffs included a shock tariff rate of 46% for products imported from Vietnam. The Seattle-based company imports coffee from more than 30 countries, including Vietnam.

Notably, Starbucks (NASDAQ:SBUX) is believed to have reduced its use of hedges against coffee price shocks; however, the company has not detailed its exposure to tariffs yet. Investors may also be factoring in the increased recession risk in the U.S. and the impact on Starbucks (SBUX) of pressured consumers. There is also growing talk that backlash against U.S. products could start to impact international sales for Starbucks (SBUX) in upcoming quarters

Starbucks (SBUX) is undergoing a major transformation under new CEO Brian Niccol that appeared to have been trending in the right direction before the tariff and recession wildcards appeared. Analysts warned that Niccol and his team now face the tough decision of whether to raise coffee prices or take a significant margin hit if tariff deals are not worked out.

Shares of Starbucks (SBUX) were down 5.2% in premarket action on Friday, after plunging 11.2% on Thursday. SBUX trades at the lowest level of 2025 after mounting a three-month comeback rally before the tariff disruption. The company is expected to report earnings during the last week of April in what will be a crucial report.

data.title: Progressive trades lower as Bank of America downgrades on valuation
data.description:
The Progressive Corporation (NYSE:PGR) was trading lower after Bank of America downgraded the property and casualty insurer on valuation concerns.

Shares were trading 2.45% down Friday pre-market at $279.96.

"The S&P 500 is down 12% off its 2/19 peak and investors seem to have rotated into Progressive (NYSE:PGR) shares as U.S. tariffs get implemented and fear of recession rises. As PGR is a defensive stock and auto insurance is a cyclical business, investors should recognize today’s cyclical margin peak," said the investment bank in a research note.

"With low-single-digit upside (leaning on a healthy $8.40/sh in dividends), there isn’t enough upside to justify adding to one's PGR position here despite many desirable features of Progressive’s operations and its stock’s trading history," said the note.

Bank of America lowered its recommendation on the stock to Neutral from Buy, and reduced the price objective to $287.00 from $300.00.

The average sell-side analyst rating on PGR is Buy.

However, Bank of America's rating aligns with the Seeking Alpha authors and the Quant Rating of Hold.

data.title: Equifax downgraded to Equalweight at Barclays
data.description:
Barclays downgraded Equifax (NYSE:EFX) to Equalweight from Overweight on the basis that credit bureaus and rating agencies are most prone to negative earnings revisions as new tariffs likely boost inflation and risk a recession.

Equifax (NYSE:EFX) and TransUnion (TRU) "will clearly be hurt by a consumer recession with not many offsets," analyst Manav Patnaik wrote in a note to clients.

Even though there's potential for a big wave of mortgage refinancing, Barclays's recession scenario suggests EFX/TRU EPS will be hit more than >10% vs. Fair Isaac (FICO) by only 3%, the analyst said.

The Equalweight rating on Equifax (EFX) aligns with the SA Quant rating of Hold and diverges from the average Wall Street rating of Buy.

Equifax (EFX) stock edged down 0.2% in Friday premarket trading after sliding 6.4% on Thursday.

data.title: Bill Gross: Investors should not buy the dip, it's like ‘catch a falling knife’
data.description:
PIMCO co-founder Bill Gross said investors should stay away from buying the dip amid market turmoil.

The American investor and retired fund manager said in an email that investors should not try to “catch a falling knife,” Bloomberg reported.

“This is an epic economic and market event similar to 1971, and the end of the gold standard except with immediate negative consequences,” he said.

The Nasdaq (COMP:IND) ended Thursday’s session down 6%, while the S&P 500 (SP500) was down 4.8%, and the Dow Jones (DJI) finished the day down 4%, shedding almost 1,680 points.

Gross said this selloff, propelled by President Donald Trump’s unveiling of broad tariffs, was “a deep market event” that has little resolution in sight.

Gross said that President Trump “can’t back down anytime soon. He’s too macho for that.”

He also said he sees opportunities only in domestic stocks that could provide relatively safe dividends as interest rates fall. He named AT&T (T) and Verizon Communications (VZ). The stocks closed 1.63% and 1.97%, respectively.

But “even with these, be careful, they are approaching ‘overbought’ territory,” he said.

data.title: 'Dark days ahead:' Tech trade crushed by 'tariff economic Armageddon,' Wedbush says
data.description:
President Trump's announcement this week to levy tariffs on all countries around the world has “crushed” the tech trade via a “tariff economic Armageddon,” Wedbush Securities said on Friday.

“From the Dot.com bubble and burst to the financial crisis to Europe debt crisis to COVID-19 lows in March 2020...but never have we (or others that have covered the markets for 50 years+) seen a self-inflicted debacle of epic proportions like the Trump tariff slate over the last 36 hours,” analysts led by Dan Ives wrote in a note to clients.

Instead of a reciprocal tariff solution, the Trump Administration laid out a “convoluted set of numbers/calculations that appear to be taking each nation's trade surplus by their total imports with the US,” the analysts explained.

“The concept of taking the US back to the 1980s 'manufacturing days' with these tariffs is a bad science experiment that in the process will cause an economic Armageddon in our view and crush the tech trade, AI Revolution theme, and overall industry in the process,” the analysts added.

The tech supply chain had been based on delivering the most products to consumers all over the world at the lowest prices. But now, the economic pain brought on by these tariffs will be “hard to describe” and could take the U.S. tech industry “back a decade in the process,” while China (which retaliated on Friday with its own tariffs against U.S. goods) could move on ahead.

Tariff rates of 50% (or more) on China and 32% on Taiwan would “essentially cause a shut-off valve from the US tech landscape and in the process cause every electronic to go up 40%-50% for consumers,” with U.S.-made Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) iPhones costing $3,500 and the AI revolution — which has been led by Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA), OpenAI, Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) and others — being “significantly slowed down.”

“If these tariffs went into place at current form overall tech earnings would come down 15% at least, the supply chain will be a Rubik's Cube rivaling COVID-19 days, and the economy would go into a recession/stagflation,” the analysts added. “We assume tariff negotiations start now, otherwise dark days are ahead for tech...and US consumers pay the price for this..not a debate.”

data.title: Pfizer, GSK settle patent lawsuit over RSV vaccines
data.description:
Pfizer (NYSE:PFE) and GSK (NYSE:GSK) on Thursday agreed to end a patent lawsuit over claims that the former’s vaccine, Abrysvo for respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) allegedly violated the British pharma giant’s patent rights related to its rival shot, Arexvy.

Citing a court document filed in Delaware federal court, Reuters reported that the parties have sought to dismiss the case with prejudice, meaning the lawsuit can't be brought back. The companies didn’t immediately respond to Seeking Alpha’s requests for comments.

In 2023, the FDA approved Arexvy, which contains an adjuvant licensed from a unit of Agenus (AGEN), making it the first U.S.-cleared shot for RSV. Shortly thereafter, New York-based Pfizer (NYSE:PFE) won the FDA nod for its rival product, Arexvy.

The legal battle between the duo started in 2023 when GSK (NYSE:GSK) argued that Pfizer’s (NYSE:PFE) vaccine infringed its patents related to antigen technology, a claim the U.S. pharma giant denied.

data.title: Nutriband signs partnership agreement with Charlotte FC
data.description:
• Nutriband (NASDAQ:NTRB) has  signed an associate partnership agreement with Charlotte FC, it said on Friday.
  • The company intends to use the partnership to build visibility for its brands such as AI Tape which is manufactured locally in the Charlotte area.

data.title: Stellantis lays off workers temporarily in Michigan and Indiana amid auto sector tariff disruption
data.description:
Stellantis N.V. (NYSE:STLA) announced that it is temporarily laying off 900 workers at five U.S. facilities and pausing production at one assembly plant each in Mexico and Canada. The layoffs will affect workers at the Warren and Sterling stamping plants in Michigan, as well as the Indiana and Kokomo transmission plants and Kokomo Casting in Indiana. The decision followed shortly after the tariff reveal by President Trump earlier in the week.

In a letter sent to employees on Thursday morning, Chief Operating Office Antonio Filosa said the automaker will continue to assess the medium- and long-term effects of the tariffs on the company's operations, but needed to take some immediate actions due to demand trends. 

Shares of Stellantis (NYSE:STLA) were down 7.5% in premarket trading on Friday, after shedding 9.4% on Thursday. The auto stock is down 22% over the last six weeks amid tariff anxiety.

data.title: European markets plummet touching 9-month low amid global selloff, yields slump
